refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire volumetric or mass flow data from material handling subsystems during operation.
Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired flow data.
Display or transmit throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
Log historical throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input or output) over time and derive throughput from level-delta and cross-sectional geometry
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + spot-check mass sampling) and reconcile via statistical fusion to report throughput with confidence bounds
DP5: Instrument material transport mechanism (conveyor speed, pump displacement, bucket count) and calculate throughput from kinematic/volumetric parameters without direct material sensing
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor placement and material stream homogeneity: if material density or particle size distribution varies spatially, single-point sensing may not represent true bulk flow

Coupling between measurement modality (mass vs. volume) and material property stability: wet or compressible materials introduce density drift, requiring either continuous density correction or mode switching

Coupling between integration method (time-domain accumulation) and data quality: sensor drift, calibration drift, or signal noise can accumulate over long evaluation periods, requiring periodic recalibration checkpoints
Weak coupling to operational policy: if worksite procedures permit material bypass, recirculation, or mixing of streams, the measurement boundary must be explicitly defined to avoid ambiguity
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is logically sound and decoupled from specific sensor technology. The main risks are environmental (material property variation) and operational (stream definition), not architectural. Continuous integration supports real-time feedback and post-hoc audit trails, improving acceptance criterion verification.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Material stream is well-defined and bounded (no unmeasured bypass or recirculation during evaluation period). RISK: Worksite operational variance may violate this; recommend explicit operational protocol alignment before deployment.

ASSUMPTION: Instantaneous flow rate can be sensed with sufficient accuracy and temporal resolution to integrate meaningfully over the evaluation period. RISK: Sensor noise, calibration drift, or environmental interference may degrade integration fidelity; recommend pre-deployment sensor validation and in-situ calibration checkpoints.

ASSUMPTION: Material properties (density, particle size, moisture) remain stable or are known/corrected during evaluation. RISK: Seasonal, batch, or operational changes may introduce systematic error; recommend periodic spot-check validation against independent batch measurement.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to amortize sensor startup transients and short-term fluctuations. RISK: If evaluation period is very short (< 1 hour), integration error may exceed acceptable tolerance; recommend minimum evaluation window specification.

ASSUMPTION: Reported productivity is defined as cumulative throughput divided by elapsed time, with clear start/stop boundaries. RISK: Ambiguity in when measurement begins/ends relative to operational start/stop can bias results; recommend explicit synchronization protocol.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) whether throughput is measured in mass or volume (or both with conversion), (2) the definition of the measurement boundary (input, output, or net change), (3) the minimum evaluation period and acceptable integration error tolerance, (4) whether material property variation (density, moisture) is expected and how it will be handled, and (5) the synchronization protocol between measurement start/stop and operational start/stop. Additionally, cross-reference the worksite safety plan to confirm that sensor placement and calibration procedures do not conflict with personnel safety or operational constraints. Consider DP4 (multi-modal fusion) as a secondary option if single-modality DP1 cannot meet acceptance criteria due to environmental variability.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Physical and logical boundary encompassing all onboard sensing, actuation, and real-time data acquisition systems including flow rate measurement hardware, sensor integration, signal conditioning, and local cumulative throughput computation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput measurements to external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation) at periodic intervals or on-demand.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period parameters, sensor calibration commands, and flow measurement mode directives from external control systems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle communicates flow rate data and measurement status over network to support remote monitoring and command reception.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. Flow sensing hardware, signal acquisition, instantaneous rate computation, and integration logic are naturally co-located in vehicle systems. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry export and tasking input cross boundary. Sensor calibration and measurement algorithms remain internal.

Poor containment. NGHE Solution is a high-level orchestration/planning layer. Placing continuous sensing and real-time integration here creates tight coupling to vehicle hardware, violates separation of concerns, and forces NGHE to manage low-level sensor timing and signal conditioning—not its domain.

Poor containment. Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer. Embedding flow rate acquisition and integration here couples remote control logic to sensor hardware, introduces latency sensitivity for real-time measurement, and diffuses sensing responsibility across vehicle and remote systems.

Very poor containment. Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for hazard monitoring and alert generation. Flow rate measurement is a functional capability, not a safety function. Placing DP1 here conflates measurement with safety logic and creates high diffusion of sensing infrastructure across unrelated modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and optimal boundary for DP1 because: (1) it owns the physical sensing hardware and signal conditioning electronics; (2) instantaneous flow rate computation from sensor signals is a core vehicle capability; (3) cumulative integration over an evaluation period is a local, real-time operation best performed where data originates; (4) external systems (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume the *result* (throughput data) via Telemetry, not the sensing process itself. This preserves functional cohesion and minimizes coupling.
containment_risks: Risk: If flow sensor hardware is physically located on a separate subsystem (e.g., a remote probe or external measurement unit), the IM boundary may need to be refined to include that hardware as part of Vehicle or to define a clear sensor-to-Vehicle interface.

Risk: If evaluation period parameters or calibration commands are frequently updated by external systems, the Tasking interface may become a bottleneck; ensure Vehicle can cache and apply updates without blocking real-time measurement.

Risk: If multiple external consumers (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services) require flow rate data at different update rates or precision levels, Telemetry export may need buffering or filtering logic; ensure this remains in Vehicle and does not diffuse to consumers.

Risk: If cumulative throughput must be reset or adjusted by external command (e.g., for batch accounting), ensure Tasking interface supports reset directives without compromising real-time measurement integrity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Vehicle IM boundary owns all hardware (flow sensors, signal conditioning) and real-time computation (instantaneous rate, cumulative integration). Verification can be performed via direct measurement against calibrated reference standards during representative operations over the agreed evaluation period. Acceptance criterion (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target) is directly measurable from Vehicle telemetry output.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated entirely to Vehicle IM via DP1. External systems (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume throughput results via Telemetry interface but do not participate in measurement acquisition or integration logic. Ownership boundary is unambiguous.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 (continuous sensing + instantaneous rate computation + integration over evaluation period) is a sound and complete solution to FR (enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time). The DP specifies the measurement principle (continuous acquisition), the computational approach (integration), and the output form (cumulative throughput). No logical gaps or contradictions detected. DP is independent of implementation module choice.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Flow sensor hardware location. If the physical sensor is mounted on a remote probe, external measurement unit, or subsystem not physically co-located with Vehicle processing electronics, the IM boundary definition must be clarified. Recommend: explicitly include sensor hardware and signal conditioning as part of Vehicle IM, or define a clear sensor-to-Vehicle interface (e.g., analog/digital signal line with documented latency and noise characteristics).

Risk: Tasking interface bottleneck. If external systems (NGHE, Teleoperation) frequently update evaluation period parameters or calibration coefficients, Vehicle must cache these values and apply them without interrupting real-time measurement. Recommend: Vehicle implements a non-blocking parameter update mechanism (e.g., double-buffering or atomic swap) to prevent diffusion of synchronization logic to external systems.

Risk: Multi-consumer telemetry demand. If NGHE, Teleoperation, and Safety Services each require flow rate data at different update rates, precision levels, or aggregation windows, there is a risk that filtering/buffering logic diffuses to consumers. Recommend: Vehicle Telemetry interface exports raw instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput at a fixed, high-frequency rate; consumers apply their own filtering/decimation locally.

Risk: Cumulative reset/adjustment commands. If external systems must reset or adjust cumulative throughput for batch accounting or error correction, ensure Tasking interface supports atomic reset directives. Recommend: Vehicle implements a reset command that atomically clears cumulative counter and restarts integration; document the timing and precision of reset relative to ongoing measurement cycles.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). DP1 is naturally and optimally contained within Vehicle IM. All core responsibilities—flow sensing hardware, signal acquisition, instantaneous rate computation, and cumulative integration—are co-located in vehicle systems. Diffusion is minimal and limited to two well-defined interfaces: Telemetry (outbound export of results) and Tasking (inbound reception of parameters and commands). No evidence of DP1 logic or responsibility scattered across NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. These external systems correctly consume the *result* (throughput data) rather than participating in the measurement process. Sensor calibration and measurement algorithms remain internal to Vehicle, preserving functional cohesion and minimizing coupling.

recommended_revisions: Clarify sensor hardware ownership and location. Document whether flow sensor(s) are physically mounted on the Vehicle or on a remote/external subsystem. If remote, define the sensor-to-Vehicle interface (signal type, latency, noise characteristics, sampling rate) and confirm it is included in the Vehicle IM boundary or explicitly linked via a documented interface contract.

Specify Tasking interface protocol for parameter updates. Define the format, timing, and atomicity guarantees for evaluation period parameters and calibration commands. Recommend: Vehicle caches parameters and applies them at the start of the next measurement cycle without blocking ongoing integration.

Define Telemetry export format and rate. Specify the structure of instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput messages, the export frequency (e.g., 10 Hz, 1 Hz, on-demand), and any precision/rounding rules. Recommend: export raw data at the highest practical frequency to allow external consumers to apply their own filtering.

Document cumulative throughput reset/adjustment mechanism. If external systems require the ability to reset or adjust cumulative throughput (e.g., for batch accounting), define the Tasking command format, the timing of reset relative to ongoing measurement, and any constraints (e.g., reset only allowed when Vehicle is idle). Recommend: implement atomic reset to prevent measurement gaps or double-counting.

Validate measurement accuracy against acceptance criterion. Establish a test protocol to verify that reported productivity (cumulative throughput over evaluation period) meets or exceeds productivity_target during representative operations. Recommend: conduct calibration runs with known reference flow rates and document sensor accuracy, repeatability, and drift characteristics.

Confirm worksite safety plan compatibility. Ensure that flow measurement hardware (sensors, wiring, signal conditioning) does not introduce new hazards or violate worksite safety policies. Recommend: review sensor placement, electrical safety, and any potential interference with other vehicle systems or personnel.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The functional requirement is sound, the design parameter is logically complete, and the implementation module boundary is well-contained (0.92 score). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks identify several critical interface and operational details that must be decomposed into sub-level FRs to ensure robustness, especially given the high safety criticality context. Specifically: (1) sensor hardware ownership and signal integrity require explicit functional treatment; (2) parameter update atomicity and non-blocking behavior must be specified as functional requirements on the Vehicle IM; (3) telemetry export format and rate consistency across multiple consumers is a functional concern; (4) cumulative reset/adjustment atomicity is a functional requirement with safety implications. These are not design flaws but rather architectural details that belong at the next decomposition level to ensure traceability, verification, and safety compliance.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire continuous flow rate signal from sensor hardware with documented latency, noise, and sampling rate characteristics, and condition the signal for real-time computation within Vehicle IM.

FR1.2: Compute instantaneous volumetric or mass flow rate from conditioned sensor signal using calibration coefficients, and update the rate at a fixed high frequency (e.g., 10 Hz or higher) without blocking parameter updates.

FR1.3: Integrate instantaneous flow rate over the evaluation period to produce cumulative throughput, maintaining atomic state and supporting non-blocking parameter/calibration updates via Tasking interface.

FR1.4: Export instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput via Telemetry interface at a fixed, high-frequency rate (e.g., 10 Hz) with documented precision, rounding, and message format to support independent filtering by external consumers (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

FR1.5: Accept and apply evaluation period parameters and calibration coefficients from Tasking interface using atomic, non-blocking update mechanism (e.g., double-buffering) without interrupting ongoing measurement cycles.

FR1.6: Support atomic reset of cumulative throughput counter via Tasking interface command, with documented timing constraints and safety precautions to prevent measurement gaps or double-counting during active operations.

revise.why: Clarify and document the physical and logical boundaries of the Vehicle IM with respect to sensor hardware location, signal conditioning subsystems, and interface contracts. Specifically: (1) confirm whether flow sensor(s) are physically mounted on the Vehicle or on a remote subsystem, and if remote, define the sensor-to-Vehicle interface (signal type, latency, noise, sampling rate) as an explicit IM boundary contract; (2) document whether signal conditioning (amplification, filtering, analog-to-digital conversion) is performed on-board the Vehicle or externally, and ensure all measurement-critical hardware is included in or explicitly linked to the Vehicle IM; (3) define the Tasking and Telemetry interface protocols (message format, timing, atomicity guarantees) as formal IM contracts to guide sub-level DP design.

assumptions: Flow sensor hardware (or sensor-to-Vehicle interface) is owned and operated by Vehicle IM.
Signal conditioning (amplification, filtering, ADC) is performed within Vehicle IM or via a well-defined, low-latency interface.
Vehicle IM has sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time instantaneous rate computation and cumulative integration without blocking parameter updates.
Tasking and Telemetry interfaces are asynchronous and non-blocking; Vehicle does not wait for external system acknowledgment before continuing measurement.
Evaluation period parameters and calibration coefficients are updated infrequently (not at every measurement cycle) and can be cached by Vehicle IM.
External consumers (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are responsible for their own filtering, decimation, and aggregation of telemetry data; Vehicle exports raw data at high frequency.
Cumulative throughput reset is a rare operation (e.g., at batch boundaries) and can be constrained to occur only when Vehicle is idle or during a safe state.
open_risks: Sensor hardware location and signal integrity: If flow sensor is mounted remotely or on a subsystem not co-located with Vehicle processing electronics, latency, noise, and synchronization issues may degrade measurement accuracy. Mitigation: explicitly define sensor-to-Vehicle interface contract and validate signal quality during integration testing.

Parameter update atomicity: If Tasking interface updates are not properly synchronized with measurement cycles, calibration coefficients or evaluation period parameters may be applied mid-cycle, causing discontinuities or double-counting. Mitigation: implement double-buffering or atomic swap mechanism; document timing guarantees.

Multi-consumer telemetry demand: If NGHE, Teleoperation, and Safety Services require different update rates or precision levels, there is risk of diffusion of filtering logic to Vehicle IM. Mitigation: Vehicle exports raw data at fixed high frequency; consumers apply their own filtering locally.

Cumulative reset safety: If cumulative throughput reset is not atomic or is not properly synchronized with ongoing measurement, there is risk of measurement gaps, double-counting, or loss of data. Mitigation: implement atomic reset command; document constraints and timing relative to measurement cycles; consider safety implications (e.g., prevent reset during active material flow).

Measurement accuracy validation: Acceptance criterion (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target) depends on sensor accuracy, calibration, and integration algorithm. If sensor drift, non-linearity, or environmental factors are not characterized, reported throughput may not meet target. Mitigation: conduct calibration runs with known reference flow rates; document sensor accuracy, repeatability, and drift characteristics; establish periodic recalibration schedule.
evidence_needed: Sensor hardware specification: manufacturer datasheet, accuracy/repeatability/drift characteristics, operating range, environmental constraints, mounting location, signal output type (analog/digital), sampling rate.
Signal conditioning design: circuit schematic, amplification/filtering specifications, ADC resolution and sampling rate, latency from sensor to Vehicle processor.
Tasking interface protocol specification: message format, parameter update timing, atomicity guarantees, error handling, constraints on when updates are allowed.

Telemetry interface protocol specification: message format, export frequency, precision/rounding rules, data structure (instantaneous rate, cumulative throughput, timestamp, evaluation period metadata).
Cumulative reset mechanism specification: command format, timing constraints, safety precautions, atomic guarantees, documentation of reset behavior relative to ongoing measurement cycles.

Measurement accuracy validation test plan: calibration procedure, reference flow rate sources, acceptance criteria, test duration, environmental conditions, sensor drift monitoring, periodic recalibration schedule.
Safety analysis: hazard assessment for sensor hardware placement, electrical safety review, interference analysis with other vehicle systems, worksite safety plan compatibility.
